era_context: The year 1357 falls in the mid-14th century, a period of significant political and military upheaval across Eurasia. The Hundred Years' War between England and France was in a phase of English military dominance, following the English victory at Poitiers (1356). The Ottoman Empire was consolidating power in Anatolia and beginning its expansion into the Balkans. In China, the Yuan Dynasty was in its final decades, with regional warlords competing for supremacy. The Delhi Sultanate controlled much of India. This was an era of demographic recovery from the Black Death, military innovation, and the early stirrings of the Renaissance.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (pre-Columbian records fragmentary)
Central Asia (limited sources beyond Ottoman and Chinese perspectives)

events.title: Hundred Years' War: Ransom of King John II of France
events.description: King John II of France, captured at the Battle of Poitiers in 1356, is ransomed and released in 1357 after payment of a substantial ransom. The ransom is one of the largest financial transactions of the medieval period and reflects the military and political dominance of England in this phase of the Hundred Years' War. John II's release and the subsequent negotiations lead to the Treaty of Brétigny (1360), which temporarily ends the war. The ransom also reflects the broader economic impact of the war on France.
key_figures: Edward, the Black Prince (Edward III's son, captor)
events.certainty_note: The ransom of King John II in 1357 is well-documented in contemporary chronicles and royal records. The date is secure.
cross_references: 1356-001 (Battle of Poitiers, capture of John II)

events.title: Ottoman Empire expands in Anatolia and Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Orhan I (r. 1323–1362), continues its military expansion in Anatolia and begins serious incursions into the Balkans. By 1357, Ottoman forces have captured several important Balkan fortresses and are threatening Byzantine territories. This expansion is part of a broader Ottoman strategy to become a major imperial power, which will be fully realized over the next century.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the late 1350s is attested in both Ottoman and Byzantine sources, but specific events for 1357 are not always precisely dated. The broader trajectory is secure.

events.title: Zhu Yuanzhang defeats rival warlords in southern China
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang, the future Hongwu Emperor, continues his military campaigns to consolidate control over southern China. By 1357, he has defeated several major rivals and is the dominant power in the Yangtze River valley. His rise to power is part of the broader collapse of the Yuan Dynasty and the emergence of the Ming Dynasty, which will rule China from 1368 to 1644.
events.certainty_note: Zhu Yuanzhang's military campaigns in the 1350s are well-documented in Chinese historical records, but specific events for 1357 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Vijayanagara Empire consolidates power in southern India
events.description: The Vijayanagara Empire, under the Sangama dynasty, continues its expansion and consolidation of power in southern India. By 1357, the empire has become a major regional power, controlling much of the Deccan and southern India. The empire is known for its administrative efficiency, military innovation, and patronage of Hindu culture and architecture. Specific events for 1357 are not well-documented in surviving sources, but the broader trajectory of expansion is clear.
events.certainty_note: Vijayanagara's expansion in the 1350s–1360s is attested in inscriptions and temple records, but specific events for 1357 are not precisely dated. The broader trajectory is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of King John II's ransom and release has been subject to minor scholarly debate; some sources place it in 1356 or 1358, though 1357 is the consensus date. The specific events of Ottoman expansion in 1357 are not always precisely dated; the broader trajectory of Ottoman expansion is secure, but individual campaigns and conquests are sometimes assigned to different years in different sources. Zhu Yuanzhang's specific military movements in 1357 are difficult to pinpoint to a single year.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1357 is strong for Western Europe (the ransom of King John II is well-documented in contemporary chronicles and royal records). Chinese sources are substantial but often compiled after the fact. Ottoman sources are less precise for this specific year. Indian sources are primarily epigraphic and architectural, which limits precision for specific years. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas remain largely absent from surviving written records.
